nativeRange.habitat: Blackwater forest streams and river margins with overhanging vegetation; surface-oriented in slow to still water with heavy leaf litter and tannin-stained water

decorPreferences: floating plants (Amazon frogbit, water lettuce, salvinia) to diffuse light and provide surface cover
driftwood and leaf litter for tannins
gentle surface flow only — strong current stresses this species

diet.notes: The Black-winged Hatchetfish is a specialized surface feeder. In the wild it preys on small insects, invertebrates, and zooplankton that fall onto or rest at the water surface. In captivity, it should be offered small foods that float: wingless or flightless fruit flies (Drosophila), finely crushed floating flake, freeze-dried or frozen bloodworms, micro-pellets that remain near the surface, and baby brine shrimp presented at the surface layer. Foods that sink are generally ignored. Feeding small amounts two or three times daily and offering variety supports long-term health.

goodWith: small peaceful tetras (rummy nose, neon, ember, cardinal) in mid/lower zones
corydoras and other small bottom-dwelling catfish
apistogramma and other small dwarf cichlids
avoidWith: boisterous or fast-moving surface fish (danios, rainbowfish) that outcompete for surface food
aggressive or large fish that trigger chronic stress
fish requiring hard alkaline water — incompatible water chemistry

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by Tsunamicarlos, 650x511. Group of Carnegiella marthae at the water surface showing the deep hatchet keel and dark lateral 'wing' stripe on translucent bodies; best represents this schooling surface-dweller as kept.
Photo by Dulcimerist, 550x330. Sharp lone specimen on blue background most clearly showing the diagnostic single dark horizontal flank stripe and translucent-silver body.
Photo by Tsunamicarlos, 403x333. Closer group view showing keel body detail and lateral stripe on multiple individuals; slightly soft, kept as variety extra.

summary: The Black-winged Hatchetfish is a tiny, peaceful surface-dweller from Amazonian blackwater streams, reaching around 3.5 cm as an adult. Its signature dark wing stripe and genuine gliding ability make a tight-fitting lid essential. It requires soft, acidic water and should be kept in groups of at least eight.
careNotes:
Carnegiella marthae is the smallest member of Gasteropelecidae and one of two truly tiny hatchetfishes that appear periodically in the specialty trade (the other being the Pygmy Hatchetfish, C. myersi). It should not be confused with the Marbled Hatchetfish (C. strigata, which has a bold marbled black-and-cream pattern and is slightly larger) or the Silver Hatchetfish (Gasteropelecus sternicla, a larger genus with a plain silver body and a distinctly different profile). C. marthae is identified by the single dark horizontal stripe running along the lower flank — the 'wing' — and an otherwise translucent-silver body.

Distinguishing trait: unlike most fish that merely jump, hatchetfishes (especially Carnegiella) are documented to use rapid pectoral-fin beats to generate powered gliding flight over the water surface to escape predators. Even a small gap in a lid is sufficient for escape. A tight-fitting glass or polycarbonate cover is non-negotiable. Surface agitation should be minimal.

This is a strict blackwater species requiring soft (gH 0–8), acidic (pH 4.5–6.5), very low-mineral water. Reverse-osmosis water remineralized lightly (or left unmineralized) with tannin additives (Indian almond leaves, alder cones, peat filtration) is ideal. Hard or alkaline water causes rapid deterioration; this species is unforgiving of water-chemistry errors. Temperature stability around 24–26 °C is preferred.

Keep in schools of at least 8 — preferably 10–15 — to achieve confident behavior and reduce stress. Solitary specimens or small groups are often found hiding and rarely visible. C. marthae occupies the top few centimetres of the water column almost exclusively; a long, shallow tank footprint (60 cm minimum length) is preferred over tall designs. Floating plants (frogbit, salvinia, water lettuce) provide essential surface cover and diffuse light intensity, helping the fish feel secure.

Feeding requires patience: foods must float. Flightless fruit flies, small floating pellets, and freeze-dried/frozen invertebrates at the surface are recommended. Sinking foods are ignored. Feed at the same time each day; this species has modest metabolic demands but benefits from consistent routine.

New arrivals are often stressed from shipping; a quarantine period in well-conditioned blackwater with low light is strongly advised. Ich and Velvet are the most common disease threats; the species is sensitive to medications — use half-doses and prefer heat treatment for ich where possible.
breedingNotes: Spawning in captivity is rare and considered difficult. C. marthae is an egg-scatterer; when spawning has been observed, eggs are deposited among floating plants or dropped to the substrate. No parental care is given and adults will eat eggs. A species-only breeding tank with very soft, acidic water (pH 5.0–6.0, gH < 2), live surface foods, and a layer of fine-leaved floating plants like java moss or hornwort suspended near the surface is the standard approach. Eggs hatch in approximately 30 hours at 27 °C; fry are tiny and require infusoria or Paramecium initially, then baby brine shrimp nauplii. Success rate is low commercially; most specimens in the trade are wild-caught.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Carnegiella marthae
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided pH range (4.5–6.5), hardness (0–90 ppm / 0–5 dH), temperature (23–28°C), adult size (~3.5 cm), native range (Rio Orinoco and Amazon drainages: Venezuela, Colombia, Brazil, Peru), habitat (blackwater streams), diet (surface invertebrates), behavior, breeding notes, and species identification guidance distinguishing C. marthae from C. strigata and Gasteropelecus spp.

additional.name: FishBase — Carnegiella marthae
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (max TL 3.5 cm), distribution (Colombia, Venezuela, Brazil, Peru — upper Amazon and Orinoco drainages), taxonomy (Gasteropelecidae, Characiformes), habitat (surface of blackwater streams and rivers), and diet (invertebrates, surface insects). Confirmed family Gasteropelecidae and order Characiformes.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Freshwater Hatchetfish Care
additional.notes: Trade care perspective: confirmed tight lid requirement (gliding escape), schooling minimum of 6–10, floating food requirement, soft acidic water, surface-only swim zone. Noted that all hatchetfish should be purchased in groups of at least 6 and that the escape/gliding behavior is species-confirmed. Temperature cited as 74–82°F (23–28°C).

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Hatchetfish species guide
additional.notes: Supplementary source for species identification (distinguishing C. marthae from C. strigata and Gasteropelecus sternicla), escape/gliding biology, care requirements (blackwater, floating plants, surface feeding), and group size guidance.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Hatchetfish of the Amazon
additional.notes: Historical and biological reference: confirmed gliding flight mechanism (pectoral-fin powered, not passive), species-level taxonomic review of Gasteropelecidae, wild habitat observations (surface film of tannin-stained forest streams), and diet composition in wild collections. Supported intermediate care rating due to strict water chemistry demands.

fish.breedingNotes: The Black-winged Hatchetfish is an egg-scatterer that spawns among floating plants in very soft, acidic water. No parental care is provided, and adults will consume both eggs and fry if not removed. The fry are extremely small and require infusoria or Paramecium as first foods, followed by baby brine shrimp nauplii as they grow. Most specimens in the aquarium trade are wild-caught; successful captive breeding remains uncommon.
